posts.name: Al3ks V3y
posts.cooked:
Sushi night with a 2 and 5 year old (and 4 week old!) on a cold rainy day called for a nice Burgundy. This one really delivered! Get these while you can…

  • 2017 Domaine Jean-Marc / Thomas Bouley Volnay - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Volnay (4/23/2023)
    This is so nicely in the zone and really pushing 1er Cru quality.

    So much on the nose - cherries, raspberry, blood orange, florals. Palate just has all the red fruits in harmony with a touch of white pepper and damp earth. Not super complex, but there is some depth here and just perfect balance. Nice acidity and light powdery tannins on the finish.

    Great wine and easy to see why Thomas is such a rising star. (92 points)

Posted from CellarTracker

link_counts.title: Community Tasting Note - 2017 Domaine Jean-Marc / Thomas Bouley Volnay - CellarTracker

posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked:
Thanks sounds great.
Love these wines. Have some 17 Pommard and Fremiers.
Time to bust one perhaps?

posts.name: Al3ks V3y
posts.cooked:
Pommard is probably worth opening. these tend to drink a couple years older than they are in practice so opening young seems to work out well.

Not sure on the Fremiers, but I’m hesitant since I only have 1 bottle

posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.cooked:
Aleks_V:

Pommard is probably worth opening. these tend to drink a couple years older than they are in practice

The Rugiens is more structured than the Fremiers, and more built for the long haul. That’s the 1er Cru though, not the village. If you are in NY… whenever the 2017s are not shut down (which I assume is now?)… I’m happy to open a 2017 Rugiens and 2017 Fremier if you pick up the food check

posts.name: Al3ks V3y
posts.cooked:
Shan_A:
Aleks_V:

Pommard is probably worth opening. these tend to drink a couple years older than they are in practice

The Rugiens is more structured than the Fremiers, and more built for the long haul. That’s the 1er Cru though, not the village. If you are in NY… whenever the 2017s are not shut down (which I assume is now?)… I’m happy to open a 2017 Rugiens and 2017 Fremier if you pick up the food check

I will happily take you up on that and can reciprocate for DC!

posts.name: Josh Grossman
posts.cooked: If you ever find yourself in Ohio and want to do a horizontal Bouley Vonay off, let me know, as I have too much Réyane et Pascal Bouley.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Thanks Aleks. Have been drinking a few '17 Volnay V.V. of late. Right in the zone!

posts.name: Mike Finberg
posts.cooked: Had a 17 Chenes a few weeks ago that was very disjointed… was pretty disappointing.

posts.name: Justin Izzo
posts.cooked: That’s a bummer, not least because I just picked up a bottle, myself. Think it just needs time?

posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked: I had the ‘16 of this the other day and it was among the most satisfying red burgs (regardless of level) in recent memory. Just superb with a huge runway to improve.

posts.name: Mike Finberg
posts.cooked: Hard to say. I’ve been generally happy with other Bouley bottles I’ve tried but this was the 2nd 17 Chenes in a row that was not great.

posts.name: David_Bloch
posts.cooked: Opened one yesterday based on your note. A very, very good and true to Volnay Volnay. I would say the wine needs a bit more time given the structure, but with the right meal this is clearly approachable, and more importantly, quite enjoyable. Bought several bottles from a DC shop and went back to look for more earlier, but alas, just showing 2018s (all Volnay) and a 2019 (Pommard) in their inventory.

posts.name: Ilkka |_ |_| mme
posts.cooked: Sorry for the thread drift but I wonder if anyone has experience with Bouley’s Beaune Reversées? I have a bottle of the 2012 and am intrigued to pop the cork.

posts.name: Justin Izzo
posts.cooked: Thanks, Mike. I may give my lone bottle till 2030 or so and hope for the best. Meanwhile, perhaps I’ll pick up some of this village (or the VV bottling) so I can have some basis for comparison.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
2012 was a hail vintage, not sure if Reversées was impacted.

But it should be quite ready to go nonetheless. Soils are sandy and it’s the supplest, most demonstrative 1er cru in his range.

posts.name: Ilkka |_ |_| mme
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

2012 was a hail vintage, not sure if Reversées was impacted.

But it should be quite ready to go nonetheless. Soils are sandy and it’s the supplest, most demonstrative 1er cru in his range.

Major thanks! Good to know there is no need to wait.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
Drank the ‘17 clos de la cave last night and thought it was fabulous. Side-by-side with the Hudelot Noellat Chambolle of the same vintage, the Bouley was the more complex and delicious wine.
Alex

posts.name: Daniel Paik
posts.cooked: Had the 17 volnay vv several months ago and felt it could definitely use more time, quite dense and maybe felt a little blocky. 18 pommard drinking really easy now

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: The 2012 Domaine Jean-Marc Bouley Pommard 1er Cru Cuvée Leonie is a wonderful wine, drinking beautifully now. I suspect the Beaune Reversées should be in the zone.

posts.name: Erlend @ntonsen
posts.cooked: Any wisdom on the current drinkability of the ‘18 Fremiers, anyone?

posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.cooked: Thanks. Someone else suggested this may need more time, but I am keen to try one.

posts.name: Steven Dilley
posts.cooked:
IlkkaL:

Sorry for the thread drift but I wonder if anyone has experience with Bouley’s Beaune Reversées? I have a bottle of the 2012 and am intrigued to pop the cork.

FWIW, had a bottle of the '12 Caillerets last night… crazy good.

posts.name: Al3ks V3y
posts.cooked: Bunch of Bouley on winebid tonight…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Probably want to decant if you do attempt it… It’s quite a big beast but I don’t think actively unapproachable. Just tend to prefer them with 10+ years on the clock personally.

posts.name: James Smallwood
posts.cooked: I second this - a fantastic wine (the Leonie)
